question: How to ensure test point accessibility in PCB design?
options: A
Test points can be distributed near each power module, which is convenient for debugging but may obscure the device and increase the difficulty of probe positioning
B
All test points can be centrally arranged under a dense component area to save layout area but not to facilitate probe contact
C
Ensure that test points are evenly distributed and properly spaced to avoid obscuration by components, and reserve probe space
D
Test points should be arranged in the corners of the PCB for easy operation, even if the signal is routed far away
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What manufacturability issues should be considered in panelization?
options: A
Different functional boards can be combined at will, but the size should be the same to avoid interfering with the manufacturing process
B
The splitting method, the consistency of the panel orientation and the safety distance of edge devices should be considered to avoid manufacturing interference
C
Process edge settings can be omitted to maximize effective area and only add seams when necessary
D
Rotate the board arbitrarily to fill the panel area, even if the orientation is inconsistent, it will not affect the soldering process
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to reduce the risk of cold solder joints during SMT?
options: A
Add flux storage areas on all pads and use a strategy to maximize solder paste coverage to ensure solder joint strength
B
Keep the copper skin around the pad well connected, but don't worry about island copper or reflow curve control
C
Increase warm-up time and increase peak temperature to improve soldering strength, even if the pad design is slightly flawed
D
Ensure that the pad design is reasonable, avoid island copper skin, and use appropriate reflow temperature curves
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to place key test nodes in DFT design?
options: A
Key nodes should be placed at the bottom of the component to save space and connect external probes through blind holes
B
Key test nodes can be placed in the signal terminal area to facilitate uniform leading, even if the path is long
C
The key test nodes should be close to the signal source, and the wiring should be as short and straight as possible to avoid crossing sensitive areas
D
All test points are centrally arranged on the debug panel, even if they are far from the actual functional unit
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What is the impact of plugged vs. non-plugged vias in manufacturing?
options: A
Uncovered oil is suitable for all high-density BGA regions to improve pad thermal stability and prevent solder bead formation
B
The through-hole cap oil method reduces electrical interference, is suitable for high-speed signal channels, and simplifies the production process
C
Uncovered oil is more conducive to reliability control under BGA, but it will increase the process cost and test difficulty
D
Through-hole cover oil prevents solder leakage and is suitable for under BGA; Non-cap oil costs low but high welding risk
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to optimize layout to reduce ICT fixture complexity?
options: A
Unify device orientation, centralize test point areas, and avoid blocking fixture probe paths
B
Evenly distribute the test vias in each layer of signal, which can effectively alleviate the load problem of the fixture circuit
C
All devices should be placed in the middle of the board so that the fixture cannot reach the test points in the area at both ends
D
Test points should be shared with the debug interface to save space, but may increase the risk of interference
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why should components be kept away from panel break-away areas?
options: A
The broken edge can increase the assembly strength, make the component weld more firmly, and improve the overall reliability
B
The broken edge of the panel has a good heat dissipation path, which is conducive to the layout and stability improvement of high-power devices
C
Flexible wiring in the broken edge area helps optimize layout density and production efficiency
D
The mechanical stress at the broken edge of the panel is large, which can easily cause the pad to fall off or the device to be damaged
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What factors determine minimum trace width in DFM?
options: A
Automatic wiring settings according to standard spacing, maintaining consistency without considering current density
B
Determined by the number of wiring and package density, ignoring process and electrical characteristics
C
Priority is given to the number of trace layers and the number of vias, and then unified into the default process standard
D
Determined comprehensively according to PCB process capability, current demand and EMC requirements
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to improve PCB AOI inspection pass rate?
options: A
Increase the area and number of copper foil to improve the contrast of image recognition and enhance the machine recognition effect
B
Keep the pads neat, the silk screen prints clear, and the device orientation consistent to avoid the obstruction of special-shaped packages
C
Reduce the number of test points and centralize the layout to reduce the interference area of image recognition
D
All devices use the same package height and form factor to facilitate uniform inspection algorithms
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why should test points not be placed under BGA arrays?
options: A
It is conducive to the arrangement of test points close to the signal source, which improves the detection speed but sacrifices accuracy
B
Avoids wiring interference, aids in EMC control, but slightly reduces probe sensitivity
C
It is not convenient to contact the probe and the detection accuracy is poor, which affects the reliability of the test
D
Flying probe testing can be used to compensate for the point limitation, but the cost and cycle time increase significantly
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to ensure test points do not affect signal integrity in high-speed PCBs?
options: A
Test points should be evenly distributed at both ends of all differential lines to facilitate simultaneous measurements
B
Avoid setting up test points directly on high-speed or differential lines, and use via lead-out tests or use dedicated probe interfaces
C
Use long leads to connect test points to the main line, which is beneficial for wiring separation and signal stability
D
Improve signal observation accuracy by adding test pins at the ends of all high-speed lines
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to avoid false positives in Automated Optical Inspection (AOI)?
options: A
Fine-pitch pads should be used to reduce image interference and AI analysis models should be used to improve inspection efficiency
B
Avoid screen printing blocking the pad, ensure uniform solder joints, and standardize the placement direction of the device
C
All components should be placed in a mirror image so that the front and back sides can be detected and synchronized
D
Use a special colored solder mask to enhance image contrast and reduce identification error rate
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What DFM concerns arise in multi-board panel design?
options: A
Ensure power integrity design priority and use double-sided mounting to save space
B
Heat release holes are set up on the edge of each board to allow the temperature to be balanced during the reflow process
C
Pay attention to the consistency of the frame between the boards, the wiring does not cross the board seams, and the unity of the board direction to improve the placement efficiency
D
Minimize the size of the board and increase the density of the panel to improve the board yield and assembly compactness
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why is it recommended to add auxiliary test structures for BGA chips?
options: A
All BGA packages have built-in test probes, eliminating the need for external test design
B
Reduces BOM costs and avoids redundant test pins and related layouts
C
To improve high-frequency performance, additional parallel decoupling networks are required for auxiliary testing
D
BGA is not visible under the bulb, and it is recommended to use edge test points or built-in scan chains to assist in verification
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to control minimum annular ring size for vias in DFM?
options: A
Set the ring width to any size, as long as it can pass the electrical connectivity test
B
Based on the principle of matching the width of the pad and the trace, and ensure that the silk screen prints do not overlap
C
Set the minimum ring width of the via according to the machining capacity to avoid open loops or reduced reliability
D
Prioritize ringless vias to reduce the impact of drilling errors on signal integrity
E
None of the above options are correct

question: What physical constraints should ICT test point layout satisfy?
options: A
It should be arranged in the middle of the high-speed signal path to facilitate timing testing and matching
B
Keep the test point open and away from the ground plane to allow flexible contact of the probe
C
The test points should be well spaced and unobstructed, and located at the bottom of the board to facilitate the contact of the fixture probe
D
Evenly distribute the test points in each layer to ensure minimal interference during testing
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why avoid placing test points near SMT inductors?
options: A
There is a stable pressure difference at both ends of the inductor, which is convenient for the probe to accurately measure the relevant parameters
B
Magnetic coupling is easy to form around the inductor, and the proximity of the test point may reduce the signal reliability
C
False measurements are caused by the small pad area, making it difficult for the probe to contact reliably
D
The magnetic field around the chip inductor is strong, which may affect the test accuracy, and a sufficient distance should be maintained
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to improve boundary scan test coverage in design?
options: A
Use devices that support IEEE 1149.1 to ensure that the scan chain is complete and that the TAP port routing is reserved
B
Configure pull-up resistors for all common IO pins for enhanced controllability and observability
C
Unified component packaging models to facilitate path calculation and matching during test chain networking
D
Allocate multiple sets of power domains in advance to avoid conflicts in the scan chain between different chips
E
None of the above options are correct

question: In multi-layer PCBs, how can the integrity of the ground layers be controlled to reduce the EMI risks of high-speed signals?
options: A
Layers should be stacked symmetrically as much as possible, and multi-point grounding should be used to improve grounding efficiency
B
Use multiple power layers to compensate for each other's radiation, enhancing common-mode current path consistency
C
The stratum should continuously cover the bottom of the high-speed line to avoid splitting and gaps affecting the return path
D
The introduction of impedance matching networks to replace continuous strata can improve electromagnetic compatibility
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why is it not recommended to place test points directly beneath the BGA chip?
options: A
Causes test probe shift, resulting in contact failure and reduced fixture consistency
B
The space below is limited, which is not conducive to probe contact and increases the difficulty of maintenance
C
Visual detection cannot be performed under BGA, which increases the difficulty of AOI or manual detection
D
There is a heat source distribution at the bottom of the package that can interfere with testing and compromise probe performance
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why should test points not be placed near the edge of large copper pours?
options: A
The edge of the copper skin may form a floating potential difference, resulting in signal noise interference and detection anomalies
B
The probe is easy to shift the test position, resulting in a significant increase in the complexity of fixture debugging
C
The edge is unstable and can easily cause false welding or inaccurate testing, so the test point should be moved inward
D
There is a concentration of thermal stress in the edge area of the copper foil, which may cause inconsistent welding quality
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to reserve debug points for functional testing during PCB design?
options: A
Test pads can be added to non-critical signals to reduce wiring pressure
B
Arrange common test buses between all modules to facilitate parallel debugging operations
C
Only some fault-prone devices are arranged next to the test holes to save space
D
Jumper holes or pads should be reserved on key signal lines to facilitate debugging and measurement
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why should the pad size for test points be limited?
options: A
The large size of the pad can lead to instability in the probe contact area and affect the accuracy of the test resistance measurement
B
Oversized pads may affect the stacking structure of the PCB and cause signal path deformation
C
Oversized pads can lead to uneven solder distribution or unstable contact with test probes
D
The large pad area is prone to absorbing too much heat, causing local temperature rise to cause soldering deviation
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to avoid soldering anomalies on test points during reflow?
options: A
Thermal barrier grooves can be set up on both sides of the pad to stabilize the heat distribution of reflow soldering
B
Control flux ratio to reduce solder paste spillage and keep surfaces clean
C
Adopt solder mask cover or dedicated padless structure to control solder flow
D
Increase the diameter of the test point to create a stable solder ball adsorption area
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to improve flying probe test efficiency through layout?
options: A
Test points should be coplanar with the high-current path to enhance structural stability
B
Place the test points in the high-speed signal path to facilitate fixture identification
C
Test points should avoid occlusion, be concentrated, and easy to plan probe paths
D
Uniform test point shape and size and alignment close to the edge of the board can help reduce assembly error rates
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why use non-contact test methods for high-speed boards?
options: A
The signal integrity of the high-speed board is high, and there are no special requirements for the test method
B
The contact area of the probe is limited, which can easily lead to false judgments and increase signal delay
C
Probe contact may interfere with high-speed signals, and scanning or boundary testing techniques should be used
D
Using probe testing helps to measure power consumption and ground potential difference at the same time
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to prevent oxidation from affecting long-term testability of test points?
options: A
Use through-hole construction to improve mechanical strength, facilitate probe contact, and reduce maintenance costs
B
Minimize surface preparation steps to reduce process complexity
C
Immersion gold process or tin plating can be used to protect the test point surface to improve the stability of the test point
D
Arrange test points close to high-frequency devices to reduce contact impedance fluctuations
E
None of the above options are correct

question: Why standardize component orientation for testability?
options: A
It is conducive to the unified optimization of chip packaging parameters and the reduction of mechanical stress
B
It is convenient for the inspection system to identify device types and improve the efficiency of placement and inspection
C
It can reduce the risk of assembly stress concentration caused by thermal expansion and contraction
D
Helps reduce wiring length and improve parasitic parameter consistency
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to reduce signal interference caused by clustered test points?
options: A
Contact reliability can be enhanced by increasing the test point area to improve anti-interference ability
B
Add ground wire connections between all test points to form a complete loop
C
Test points should be kept away from sensitive analog signal lines and avoid backflow path conflicts
D
Unify the test point hierarchy to improve electrical symmetry and suppression
E
None of the above options are correct

question: How to avoid placing test points in depaneling slots?
options: A
Define the test area separately at the pad layer and set up the multi-identification marker area
B
Plan the panel boundaries in advance to avoid the proximity of test points to V-CUT or rat bite grooves
C
Unify all panel structure parameters to reduce the impact of fixture and test errors
D
An auxiliary grounding loop is set up at the four corners of each daughter board to enhance stability
E
None of the above options are correct
